title: How Acid Rain Forms and Harms Ecosystems

imageCredit.creator: Origins.gif: The original uploader was NHSavage at English Wikipedia. derivative work: Zazou (talk)

lesson.learn:
Acid rain forms when sulfur dioxide and nitrogen oxides react in the atmosphere with water, oxygen, and sunlight. The result is sulfuric and nitric acids that fall with rain, snow, fog, or dry particles. It can make lakes more acidic, damage forests, and wear away buildings and monuments.

Acid rain is a good example of how pollution can travel far from its source. Smoke from power plants or factories does not stay local; it can turn into an atmospheric chemical problem that affects ecosystems many kilometers away.
lesson.simpler: Pollution in the air can turn into acids that fall with rain. That acidic water can damage lakes, forests, and stone buildings.
lesson.keyTakeaway: Acid rain is caused by air pollution that changes into acids before falling back to Earth.
lesson.deeperDive:
Once sulfur dioxide and nitrogen oxides are released, winds can carry them long distances before they transform. In the atmosphere, they react with oxidants and moisture to form acidic compounds. Sensitive lakes and soils are affected first because they have less buffering capacity. Forests can be weakened when leaves and roots are stressed and nutrients are washed away.

The problem became much better understood in the late 20th century, and pollution controls reduced emissions in many countries. That matters because acid rain shows that environmental damage can be chemical, regional, and reversible when the source is identified and regulated.
lesson.funFact: Acid rain can travel far from the power plant or factory that created it.

people: Scientists studying atmospheric chemistry - the people who explained acid rain
Environmental regulators - the teams that reduced emissions
Foresters - people who observed tree damage
Hydrologists - experts tracking lake and soil acidification
places: Eastern North America - historic acid rain region
Northern Europe - major cross-border pollution area
The Jizera Mountains - a known forest damage site
Industrial power corridors - places where emissions were high
years: 1970s - acid rain becomes a major environmental issue
1990s - emissions controls begin to reduce sulfur pollution
21st century - many regions see partial recovery
Present day - monitoring continues because ecosystems recover slowly
concepts: Sulfur dioxide - an acid rain precursor
Nitrogen oxides - another acid rain precursor
Atmospheric oxidation - chemistry in the air
Buffering capacity - a lake or soil’s resistance to acidification
Transboundary pollution - pollution that crosses borders
